coredata.dc:title: One-step hydrothermal synthesis of manganese oxide nanosheets with graphene quantum dots for high-performance supercapacitors

coredata.dc:description: MnO2 is considered to be one of the promising electrode materials for supercapacitors thanks to its ultra-high theoretical capacitance value, but its actual electrochemical performance is not ideal due to its low electrical conductivity and poor stability. Herein, we find that the supercapacitor performance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)@MnO2 composite is superior to that of pure MnO2 electrode. The GQDs@MnO2 composite is obtained by a highly efficient one-step hydrothermal method, in which KMnO4 reacts with graphene oxide to produce MnO2 nanosheets anchored with GQDs in a short time. The GQD@MnO2 electrode presents high specific capacitance of 246 F g−1 at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 in Na2SO4 electrolyte, and the as-assembled asymmetric supercapacitor (GQDs@MnO2//activated carbon) exhibits superior energy density of 29.9 Wh kg−1 at power density of 538.0 W kg−1, and good cycling performance (81.3 % retention after 8000 cycles) that was far better than that of pure MnO2-based supercapacitor. The excellent supercapacitor performance of GQDs@MnO2 composite results from its enhanced electrical conductivity, good wettability and abundant available contact sites for aqueous electrolyte, which are ascribed to the intrinsic high electrical conductivity as well as the quantum confinement and edge effects of GQDs.

(a) Typical TEM image of the resulting GQDs, inset: size distribution; (b) HRTEM image, inset: its corresponding enlarged image and lattice spacing pattern; (c) PL spectra of the resulting GQDs at different excitation wavelengths; (d) top-view SEM and (e) TEM images of the GQDs@MnO2 composite; (f) SEM image of the GQDs@MnO2 composite and its corresponding element mapping information of C, O, and Mn, respectively; (g) typical SEM and (h) TEM images of the pure MnO2.
(a) Representative XRD patterns, and (b) Raman spectra the pure MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite. Inset in (b) shows the enlarged spectrum for the GQDs@MnO2 composite.

(a) Survey XPS spectrum of the GQDs@MnO2 composite. High-resolution XPS spectra of (b) C 1 s, (c) O 1 s, and (d) Mn 2p for the GQDs@MnO2 composite. High-resolution XPS spectra of (e) O 1 s, and (f) Mn 2p for the pure MnO2.

(a) CV and (b) GCD curves of the pure MnO2 electrode. (c) CV and (d) GCD curves of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode. (e) Specific capacitances at the different scan rates from 1 to 5 mV s−1. (f) Nyquist plots in a frequency range from 0.01 Hz to 100 kHz. (g) Cycling stability at a current density of 5 A g−1 for 6000 cycles.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: MnO2 is considered to be one of the promising electrode materials for supercapacitors thanks to its ultra-high theoretical capacitance value, but its actual electrochemical performance is not ideal due to its low electrical conductivity and poor stability. Herein, we find that the supercapacitor performance of graphene quantum dots (GQDs)@MnO2 composite is superior to that of pure MnO2 electrode. The GQDs@MnO2 composite is obtained by a highly efficient one-step hydrothermal method, in which KMnO4 reacts with graphene oxide to produce MnO2 nanosheets anchored with GQDs in a short time. The GQD@MnO2 electrode presents high specific capacitance of 246 F g−1 at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 in Na2SO4 electrolyte, and the as-assembled asymmetric supercapacitor (GQDs@MnO2//activated carbon) exhibits superior energy density of 29.9 Wh kg−1 at power density of 538.0 W kg−1, and good cycling performance (81.3 % retention after 8000 cycles) that was far better than that of pure MnO2-based supercapacitor. The excellent supercapacitor performance of GQDs@MnO2 composite results from its enhanced electrical conductivity, good wettability and abundant available contact sites for aqueous electrolyte, which are ascribed to the intrinsic high electrical conductivity as well as the quantum confinement and edge effects of GQDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the increasing demands for energy storage and conversion (ESC) devices to address fossil fuel shortages and environmental concerns, many efforts have been made to develop new ESC devices with excellent energy, power densities and longevity to achieve the future development of a low-carbon and sustainable economy [1–7]. Among them, supercapacitors (SCs) have attracted great research attention in recent years due to their fast charge and discharge rate, high power density, long durability, and environmental friendliness [8–14]. However, the low energy density of SCs limits their wider applications. According to the equation of SC energy density, E = 1/2CV 2, in which C is the specific capacitance that relates to the electrode materials, and V is the operation voltage that involves the electrolytes, there are two strategies for improving the energy density. One of the approaches is to choose an electrolyte with a wider voltage window for SCs, e.g. organic electrolytes, or ionic liquids. However, organic electrolytes are usually hazardous and flammable, while ionic liquids are too expensive. Thus, researchers prefer to search for electrode materials with high specific capacitance that can work in aqueous electrolytes.

Due to their charge storage mechanism, SCs can be divided into electric double-layer capacitors (EDLCs) and pseudocapacitors (PCs) [15]. Different from the ion adsorption/accumulation between the electrode/electrolyte interfaces in EDLCs, PCs arise from fast and reversible redox reactions on the surface or near the surface of pseudocapacitive materials, which generally have higher specific capacitance. To date, many transition metal oxides (e.g., RuO2, Co3O4, TiO2, SnO2, V2O5, MnO2, etc.) [16] and conducting polymers (e.g., polypyrrole, polyaniline, polythiophene, etc.) [17] have been extensively employed as pseudocapacitive electrodes. Among these materials, MnO2 is particularly outstanding as a pseudocapacitive electrode material because of its low cost, low toxicity, environmental friendliness, abundant reserves, and high theoretical specific capacitance (~1370 F g−1) [18,19]. However, there are some disadvantages including unfavorable ion transport, low conductivity (10−6–10−5 S cm−1), and poor long cycle stability, which tremendously restrict practical applications when pure MnO2 materials are directly utilized as SC electrodes. In this regard, combining MnO2 with carbonaceous materials, which have excellent electrical conductivity, high stability, and large surface area, is a feasible solution to overcome those drawbacks. In general, the MnO2 materials were complexed with various carbon structures such as porous carbon, carbon nanotubes, graphene, and their derivatives [20–22].

Recently, graphene quantum dots (GQDs), zero-dimensional graphene materials with lateral dimensions below 100 nm, have been hybridized with MnO2 nanostructures to improve the electrochemical performance of MnO2-based SCs. The improvements are due to their valuable intrinsic features from graphene and many emerging properties arising from the quantum confinement and edge effects [23–33]. Different from the abovementioned composites in which the carbon materials provided a support for the deposition of the MnO2 nanostructures, in the GQDs and MnO2 composites, the GQDs were anchored on the surfaces of the MnO2 structures. For example, Jia et al. [34] utilized plasma enhanced chemical vapor deposition to deposit graphene quantum dots on MnO2 nanosheets that grew on the surface of Ni foam. The heterostructural GQDs/MnO2 nanosheets achieved a specific capacitance up to 1170 F g−1 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 and a long cycle performance of 92.7 % after 10,000 cycles. An asymmetric SC (ASC) combining GQD/MnO2 with nitrogen-doped graphene displayed a high energy density of 118 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 923 W kg−1. Zhu et al. [35] first synthesized GQD powder from graphene oxide (GO) by a hydrothermal process and then mixed the resulting GQD powder with KMnO4 to construct a GQD@MnO2 composite, which exhibited a large specific capacitance of 208.2 F g−1. A flexible ASC configuration consisting of the GQD@MnO2 composite and activated carbon (AC) delivered a maximum energy density of 82.2 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 1.75 kW kg−1 as well as good cycling performance under the straight and bent states. Obviously, the obtained composites consisting of GQDs and MnO2 exhibited better excellent electrochemical performance than pure MnO2 materials. This should be ascribed to the abundant active sites, enhanced electric conductivity, modified density of state and energy bandgap of the GQDs/MnO2 composites. However, the whole coupling process for the GQDs and MnO2 is tedious and time-consuming.

Here, we report a highly efficient one-step method to synthesize the GQDs@MnO2 composite with the GQDs as the byproduct from KMnO4 and GO solution through the hydrothermal route at 180 °C for 1 h (Fig. 1 ). Our GQDs@MnO2 composite demonstrated a high specific capacitance of 246 F g−1 at a scan rate of 1 mV s−1 and good cycling stability with retention 80 % of the original capacitance after 6000 cycles at a current density of 1 A g−1. Furthermore, an ASC based on the obtained GQDs@MnO2 as the anode and the commercial AC as the cathode was constructed, which exhibits a superior energy density of 29.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 538.0 W kg−1 as well as a high capacitance retention of 81.3 % after 8000 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1. Obviously, compared with other processes, this work has the advantages of simple synthesis, short synthesis time and high efficiency to achieve the GQDs@MnO2 composite with excellent electrochemical performance, and provides a possibility for utilization of the GQDs@MnO2 composite in practical SC applications.

Microcrystalline graphite powder was purchased from Zhengzhou Xinmao Chemical Co. Ltd., (Zhengzhou, China), KMnO4 was purchased from Chengdu Kelong Chemical Co., Ltd., (Chengdu, China), 30 % H2O2 was purchased from Fuchen Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Tianjin, China), H2SO4 was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China), and Na2SO4 was purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., (Shanghai, China). All conventional chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized (DI) water (18 MΩ cm−1) was used during all experiments.

GO was synthesized by using the modified Hummers method [36]. Briefly, an 8:1 mixture of concentrated H2SO4/H3PO4 solution (200 mL: 25 mL) was added to a mixture of graphite flakes (5.0 g) with stirring, and produced a slight exotherm to 35–40 °C. Then, 25.0 g of KMnO4 was added slowly in port while keeping the reaction temperature below 45 °C and constantly stirred for 3 h. During the reaction, deionized water (200 mL) was gradually added with the use of an ice bath to keep the reaction temperature below 45 °C. After the reaction mixture was cooled to room temperature, 30 % H2O2 (20 mL) was added slowly. Subsequently, the mixture was sifted through a metal testing sieve and filtered through a polyester fiber membrane. The filtrate was centrifuged, and the supernatant was decanted away. The remaining materials were washed with 2 M HCl and deionized water several times. The ultimate GO powders were obtained after drying in a vacuum freeze drier at −60 °C.

In the typical synthesis procedure, a certain amount of KMnO4 (0.5–4.0 mmol) was mixed with 1 mg mL−1 GO aqueous solution (75 mL) under stirring to form a homogenous solution. Next, the total solution was transferred into a stainless-steel autoclave with a capacity of 100 mL, sealed and heated at 180 °C for 1 h. Then the autoclave was cooled to room temperature naturally. Next, the resulting product was filtered through a Buchner funnel to obtain the upper solid and the lower filtrate containing GQDs. Subsequently, the solid product was obtained by freeze-drying for 24 h, which is referred to as the composite of MnO2/GQDs (named GQDs@MnO2), and further utilized as the SC electrode. For comparison, the KMnO4 solution without GO was also hydrothermally reacted under the same conditions.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by using SU-8020 (Hitachi, Japan) and Merlin Compact (ZEISS, Germany), and JEM-2100 (JEOL, Japan), respectively. X-ray diffraction (XRD) was performed by using a Rigaku diffractometer with a Cu Kα radiation source at a scanning rate of 4° min−1 to determine the phase of the products. Raman spectrum were obtained through LabRAM HR Evolution (Horiba, France) Raman spectroscopy with a laser of 514 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was obtained by using a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha electron spectrometer with monochromatic Al Kα as the X-ray source. The FT-IR spectrum was obtained by using FTIR-spectrometer Spectrum One (PerkinElmer, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) was recorded with a fluorospectrophotometer-F97 (Lengguang, China). Atomic force microscopy (AFM) imaging was carried out by using a DI MultiMode-8 (Bruker, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed on a TG-6300 (Seiko, Japan).

The working electrode (cathode) was prepared by pressing a mixture of the as-synthesized GQDs@MnO2, acetylene black and, poly(tetrafluoroethylene) (PTFE) with a mass ratio of 8:1:1 in ethanol onto the pretreated nickel foam. A commercial AC electrode as an anode was fabricated using the same procedure. Then, the obtained electrodes were dried under vacuum at 60 °C for 24 h. A 1 M Na2SO4 solution was adopted as the electrolyte to test all the following measurements. To evaluate the electrochemical performance, electrodes were investigated in three-electrode and two-electrode systems. The three-electrode system consists of the as-prepared working electrode, a nickel mesh as the counter electrode and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, while the two-electrode system was assembled with the as-synthesized GQDs@MnO2 electrode as the cathode and the AC electrode as the anode. A suite of electrochemical measurements was studied by using a CHI 760E electrochemical workstation (Chenhua, Shanghai, China), including cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In the three-electrode configuration, the CV and GCD methods were performed in a potential window ranging from 0 to 1 V, while the sweep rates of CV were set from 1 to 5 mV s−1 and the current densities of GCD were operated from 0.1 to 0.5 A g−1. In the two-electrode system, the CV and GCD methods were performed in a potential window ranging from 0 to 2 V, while the sweep rates of CV were adopted from 1 to 20 mV s−1 and the current densities of GCD were carried out from 1 to 10 A g−1. The amplitude of the alternating current perturbation of EIS was 5 mV and the frequency range was 105 to 10−2 Hz. All electrochemical measurements were implemented at room temperature.

The specific capacitance (C s，F g−1) was calculated from the CV curve according to the following equation:
C s
=
∫ idV
ΔEνm
, where ΔE is the voltage window, i is the discharge current, V is the voltage, v is the scan rate, and m is the mass of active material in one electrode. The energy density and power density of the ASC were obtained from the corresponding equations as follows: E cell = C cell∆V 2/(2 × 3.6), P cell = 3600 × E cell/∆t, where E cell, C cell, ΔV, P cell, and Δt represent the energy density, capacitance, voltage window, power density, and discharge time, respectively.

The GO powders were prepared by using the modified Hummers method. Fig. S1a shows the SEM image of the as-prepared GO sheet. The stacked large flakes possess shiny and transparent surfaces, suggesting that the GO consists of few-layer graphene sheets that favor conglomerate during the drying of the sample. The Raman spectrum of the GO film in Fig. S1b is dominated by the two prominent D band and G band at 1360 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1, respectively. The D band comes from the breathing model of aromatic rings with the presence of defects or disordered carbon, while the G band is concerned with the in-plane phonon of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The ratio of I
D to I
G (I
D /I
G ) is usually employed to evaluate the degree of functionalization or oxidation for carbon-based materials. The I
D /I
G is approximately 0.6, which is consistent with previously reported GO. The FT-IR spectrum (Fig. S1c) suggests that the as-prepared GO sheets contain abundant functional groups, including -OH at 3413 cm−1, -COOH and/or CO at 1611 cm−1, C-O-H at 1386 cm−1 and O-C-O at 1041 cm−1. As shown in Fig. S2, the AFM image and a height profile along the red arrows indicate that the thickness of the as-prepared GO is approximately 1.1 nm, implying that its lamellar structure is composed of three-layer graphene sheets. Subsequently, the as-prepared GO solution was utilized as a precursor for the synthesis of the GQDs@MnO2 composite.

Fig. 1 shows a schematic illustration of the synthesis of the GQDs@MnO2 composite, which employed the reagents KMnO4 and GO as the precursors by a one-step hydrothermal method for 1 h. It was rarely reported that the synthesis of GQDs@MnO2 composites and GQDs could be realized by a facile one-step hydrothermal method in such a short reaction time. Fig. 2a shows the TEM image of GQDs obtained when 2 mmol KMnO4 powder was added into the GO aqueous solution in the autoclave at 180 °C for 1 h. It was found that the GQDs are spherical and monodisperse. In the inset of Fig. 2a, most of the GQDs present a relatively uniform lateral distribution of approximately 2–5 nm. Fig. 2b displays a typical well-resolved HRTEM image of individual GQDs with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which was calculated by using the software, as shown in the inset of Fig. 2b. It corresponds to the (100) in-plane lattice fringe of graphene, indicating that the GQDs hold a similar crystallinity to graphene [37]. The as-prepared GQDs (Fig. 2c) present emission profiles at different excitation wavelengths. It is noted that the emission intensity of the GQDs changed slightly with respect to the excitation wavelength. The introduced energy levels under excitation for GQDs were observed at 430 nm, 433 nm, 435 nm, 439 nm, and 446 nm with excitation wavelengths of 300 nm, 310 nm, 320 nm, 330 nm and 340 nm, respectively. This can be attributed to the decrease in number of disorder–induced states in the band gap, which leads to an increase in the number of small sp2 domains resulting blue emission at shorter wavelengths in the PL spectra of GQDs [33]. The strongest emission intensity of the GQDs observed at 435 nm upon excitation at 320 nm. Such an excitation dependent emission property clearly confirms the formation of the GQDs during the synthesis of the MnO2 nanosheets with coupling GQDs (i.e., GQDs@MnO2).

Fig. 2d shows the SEM image of the GQDs@MnO2 composite obtained from 2 mmol KMnO4 particles reacted with the GO aqueous solution in the autoclave at 180 °C for 1 h. It can be observed that sheet-like materials with lateral dimensions of 10–20 nm are vertically oriented and interconnected with each other to form network structures, which are greatly beneficial for improving the fast transport of electrons in supercapacitors [19]. These ultrathin nanosheets were confirmed to be composites of MnO2 and GQDs by subsequent TEM, XRD, and XPS analyses. The energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry mapping analyses of the GQDs@MnO2 further demonstrate the distribution of different elements including C (cyan), Mn(green), and O (red), as shown in Fig. 2f. The TEM image of the GQDs@MnO2 composite in Fig. 2e shows the lattice spacing of the fringes with 0.22, 0.24, 0.36 nm corresponding to the (015), (012), and (006) crystal planes of XRD (JCPDS No. 52-0556), respectively. Moreover, it can be clearly found that the lattice fringe of the obtained GQDs was calculated to be 0.21 nm. It is worth mentioning that some dots are distributed in the field of vision, suggesting that GQDs with size distribution of approximately 3.6–6.0 nm were formed in-situ on the surfaces of the MnO2 ultrathin nanosheets by the direct hydrothermal method. In addition, it is obvious that the TEM image of GQDs@MnO2 shows clearer visible lattice fringes compared to that of the pure MnO2 (in Fig. 2e), which may be attributed to enhancing conductivity when forming GQDs on the MnO2 nanosheets. For comparison, the KMnO4 aqueous solution without GO was hydrothermally heated at 180 °C in the autoclave, and pure MnO2 products could be achieved when extending the reaction time. Fig. 2g reveals the wall-like structures of the pure MnO2 obtained after hydrothermal reaction for 24 h. The pure MnO2 nanowalls are composed of a large quantity of thick nanosheets with lateral dimensions of 50–100 nm. As shown in Fig. 2h, the TEM image of the pure MnO2 demonstrates that the lattice spacing distances of 0.22 and 0.35 nm correspond to the (015) and (006) crystal planes based on the XRD, respectively. Obviously, GO not only greatly shortens the hydrothermal reaction time but also has a significant effect in providing nucleation sites to promote the growth of MnO2 nanosheets.

To investigate the growth mechanism of the GQDs@MnO2 composites, we changed the molar mass of KMnO4 from 0.5 mmol to 4 mmol with a certain amount of GO solution (75 mL, 1 mg mL−1) under the same hydrothermal conditions. The GO solution is initially brown and becomes aubergine by adding KMnO4. After the hydrothermal reactions, the resulting solutions and solids were tested using PL, SEM and XRD. For comparison, the GO solution without KMnO4 was hydrothermally reduced to form a cylindrical graphene gel (Fig. S3), which can be freeze dried to fabricate reduced GO (rGO) powder [38]. Obviously, the GQDs can never be synthesized without KMnO4 under the same process. Fig. S4 shows the XRD patterns of all solid products obtained from different molar masses of KMnO4 added into the GO solution. At 0.5 mmol KMnO4, a clear solution without the GQDs was achieved in the autoclave, and the solid was confirmed to be a mixture of rGO and MnCO3 powder (Fig. S5, JCPDS No.44–1472). Up to 1 mmol KMnO4, GQDs with blue fluorescence can be seen under ultraviolet irradiation, and the solid products are still MnCO3 mixture powder. With a further increase in KMnO4 from 2 mmol to 3 mmol, solid products with δ-MnO2 nanosheets and GQDs could be synthesized. However, the GQDs disappeared when the mass of KMnO4 exceeded 3 mmol. In addition, it should be noted that the hydrothermal reduction of KMnO4 to MnO2 would consume a long time without the addition of GO solution. The resulting pure MnO2 materials are larger and thicker than the GO-assisted MnO2 nanosheets. Obviously, the formation of the GQDs and MnO2 nanosheets strongly depends on the ratio of GO to KMnO4. According to these results, the reaction process for the mixed solution of GO and KMnO4 under hydrothermal conditions was explained as follows: Without adding KMnO4, the oxygen-containing functional groups in the GO sheets should be removed to build the cross-linking between some of the sheets, and the GO sheets were reduced to form rGO. This result is consistent with a previous report [38]. Due to the interaction between the GO sheets and KMnO4, at a small quantity of KMnO4, KMnO4 would be reduced to Mn2+ and form MnCO3 particles (confirmed by XRD patterns in Fig. S4) when Mn2+ bonded with CO3 2−, which originated from KMnO4 partially oxidizing carbon in a hydrothermal reaction. The resulting MnCO3 particles were surrounded by the reduced GO sheets (see Fig. S5). The morphology and microstructure of the obtained MnCO3 particles are very similar to those of MnCO3 on graphene porous frameworks achieved through the reaction between KMnO4 and GO during a hydrothermal process [39]. With increasing amounts of KMnO4, MnO2 should be produced, and the reaction can be expressed by the following equation:
2
KMnO 4
+ 3
MnCO 3
+
H 2
O ⇄ 5
MnO 2
+ 2 KOH + 3
CO 2

The GO sheets were utilized as the template to induce the formation of MnO2 nanosheets [40]. In the meantime, the GO sheets were employed as the source to synthesize the GQDs, which should be anchored on the MnO2 surfaces to form the GQDs@MnO2 composite [41]. By the addition of an excess of KMnO4 solution, another reaction occurs, as shown in the following equation [42]:
4
KMnO 4
+ 3 C +
H 2
O ⇄ 4
MnO 2
+
K 2
CO 3
+ 2
KHCO 3

It is worth noting that the reduction of KMnO4 proceeded in the presence of GO, the insoluble MnCO3 and/or MnO2 settled at the bottom of the reactor, while the soluble KHCO3 and/or K2CO3 went into the solution along with the GQDs.

Fig. 3a shows the representative XRD patterns of the pure MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite. The XRD pattern of the pure MnO2 demonstrated five peaks at 12.3°, 24.8°, 37.4°, 42.2° and 66.9°, corresponding to (003), (006), (012), (015) and (113) of δ-MnO2 (JCPDS card No.52-0556) [43], respectively. For the GQDs@MnO2 composite, its diffraction peak positions are consistent with those in the XRD pattern of δ-MnO2 [43]. However, compared with pure MnO2, the half width of peaks for the GQDs@MnO2 composite became slightly broader. This means that after loading of GQDs, no crystal transformation occurs at MnO2 and a decreasing crystallinity appears. Raman spectroscopy has become one of the most effective ways to characterize carbon-based composite materials. Fig. 3b presents the typical Raman spectra of the pure MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 composite. The strong peaks located at 501 cm−1, 575 cm−1, and 646 cm−1, which correspond to the (Mn-O-Mn) flexural vibration of MnO6 groups, the stretching vibration ν3(MnO) in the basal plane of [MnO6] sheets and the symmetric stretching vibration ν2(MnO) of MnO6 groups, respectively. These results are in agreement with a previous report about δ-MnO2 [44]. For the GQDs@MnO2 composite, in addition to the peaks of MnO2, there are two bumps at 1350 cm−1 and 1580 cm−1，which should be ascribed to the D band and G band. This result implies that the GQDs were anchored during the growth of MnO2 nanosheets. In addition, compared with that of the GO powder, the G band of the GQDs@MnO2 composite exhibited a blueshift of 20 cm−1 from 1600 cm−1 to 1580 cm−1, suggesting that the GQDs were reduced with a higher graphitization degree and that coupling of the GQDs and MnO2 nanosheets occurred.

XPS analysis was performed to further determine the surface chemistry and the composition of GQDs@MnO2. As shown in Fig. 4a, the full XPS spectrum demonstrated the existence of C, O, and Mn elements in the products, which were synthesized from the GO solution with 2 mmol KMnO4 at 180 °C for 1 h. In Fig. 4b, the C 1s spectrum exhibits three different peaks centered at 284.7 eV, 285.5 eV, and 288.4 eV, which correspond to the C-C/C=C, C−O, and O=C-O groups, respectively [45–48]. Fig. 4c shows that the O 1s peak can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 529.9 eV, 530.6 eV, and 531.6 eV, which have been assigned to the Mn-O-Mn, Mn-O-C, and Mn-O-H groups, respectively. In contrast, the O 1s peak of pure MnO2 can be fitted by only two peaks located at 529.6 and 531.3 eV, corresponding to Mn-O-Mn and Mn-O-H (see Fig. 4e). Combined with the analyses of EDX mappings and XRD patterns above, it can be further inferred that the GQDs were anchored on the surface of MnO2 nanosheets to form the GQDs@MnO2 composite. In addition, the high-resolution spectrum of Mn 2p in Fig. 4f shows peaks centered at 640.4 eV and 652.2 eV, which are considered to be Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks, respectively. The spin-energy separation at 11.6 eV between the Mn 2p3/2 and Mn2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks confirms that the Mn in the GQDs@MnO2 composite mainly exists in the chemical valence states of Mn(IV). The Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 spin-orbit peaks of pure MnO2 are centered at 642.1 and 653.9 eV in Fig. 4f, which is also attributed to the predominant presence of Mn (IV) species [49].

TGA was performed to analyze the actual content of each component in the GQDs@MnO2 composite, as shown in Fig. S6. There are three main regions in the TGA curve. A gradual decrease in weight mass below 300 °C was observed, which can be attributed to the evaporation of the physically adsorbed moisture and crystal water molecules in the GQDs@MnO2 composite, which can be estimated to be 13.3 % for water molecules in the GQDs@MnO2 composite. A significant weight loss of approximately 2.6 % in the temperature range of 300–530 °C can be ascribed to the consumption of carbon deposition in the GQDs@MnO2 composite. However, the weight loss over 530 °C can be regarded as the phase transformation of MnO2 to more stable Mn2O3 with O2 release.

Based on the abovementioned analyses, the obtained GQDs@MnO2 composite consists of δ-MnO2 which is a two-dimensional structure containing edge-sharing MnO6 octahedra and H2O molecules, and the GQDs which are anchored to the surfaces of MnO2 to form a hybrid vertical network structure through the strong hydrogen bonds between the oxygen functional groups in the GQDs and the hydroxyl groups in δ-MnO2 [50]. The vertical hybrid network structure not only can effectively present the agglomeration of MnO2, but also facilitates the fast transfer of electrons and movement of ions during the charge-discharge process [51]. Thus, the GQDs@MnO2 composite can be utilized as an ideal material for the SC electrode. Fig. 5a and c show CV curves of the pure MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 electrodes at different scan rates from 1 to 5 mV s−1 in 1 M Na2SO4 aqueous electrolyte. It was clearly observed that the two sets of curves showed visible redox reaction peaks, indicating that the electrochemical capacitances of the pure MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 electrodes are obvious pseudocapacitance features. Combined with previous literatures [16,18], the charge store mechanism of the δ-MnO2 may mainly involve two possible types: one is that alkaline cations in the electrolytes can be absorbed by the O atoms of the MnO6 octahedron due to the opposite charge properties between the alkaline cations and O atoms, and then charges could transfer to the adjacent Mn atoms, that cause the change from tetravalent to trivalent. The process limitedly happened at surface toms or a very thin layer of the δ-MnO2 nanosheets. Another is that the protons or alkaline cations such as Na+ insert into the layer spaces of the δ-MnO2 nanosheets upon reduction followed by deinsertion from the layer spaces upon oxidation as follows: MnO
2
+
C
+
+
e
−
⇄
MnOOC , where C
+ represents electrolyte cations such as H+, K+, Na+ and Li+. These redox reactions are confined to the surface or the near-surface volume of the electrode materials. Here, the redox reactions of the δ-MnO2 nanosheets in the GQDs@MnO2 composite may correspond to the surface insertion/desertion of Na+ ions from the electrolyte, confined to the surface or the near-surface volume [52,53]. With increasing scan rate, the potentials of the redox-reaction peaks shifted to the more anodic and more cathodic directions, which can be ascribed to the reinforced concentration polarization [54]. It is obvious that the CV curves of GQDs@MnO2 composite possess a much larger area and current responses than those of pure MnO2 at the same scan rates, indicating that the former has a higher specific capacitance and better capacitive behavior. The specific capacitance values of MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 were calculated from the CV curves. As shown in Fig. 5e, the specific capacitances of pure MnO2 are 182 F g−1, 167 F g−1, 164 F g−1, 162 F g−1, 150 F g−1 at scan rates from 1 to 5 mV s−1, while the values for the GQDs@MnO2 composite are 246 F g−1, 230 F g−1, 221 F g−1, 212 F g−1, and 201 F g−1, respectively. This can be attributed to the abundant edge sites provided by small GQDs and the good conductivity correlated to the Mn-O-C bond in situ forming GQDs on the MnO2 nanosheets, which is beneficial for enhancing the specific capacitance. The results of the CV curves are consistent with those reported in the literature [55]. Galvanostatic charge-discharge measurements were also carried out to investigate the electrode performance of the pure MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 composite at various current densities ranging from 0.1 to 0.5 A g−1. As shown in Fig. 5b and d, both pure MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 composite show nonlinear profiles with the plateau in the charge/discharge curves due to their pseudocapacitive features from the redox reactions. This is consistent with the observation from the CV curves. The discharge time decreases with increasing current density, suggesting that the same trend is observed for capacitance which can be compared using the discharging time. However, the charge and discharge time for the GQDs@MnO2 composite is longer than that for pure MnO2 at the same current density, indicating that the GQDs@MnO2 electrodes have higher capacitances. These results are further consistent with the results of the CV curves. Furthermore, the IR drop of 31 mV for the GQDs@MnO2 composite is much lower than that (54 mV) for pure MnO2 at 1 A g−1, meaning faster migration of ions, higher electrical energy and smaller total resistance of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode because of the coupling of GQDs on the MnO2 surfaces.

To further investigate the electrochemical performance of pure MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 electrodes, EIS results which are usually employed to analyze the ion diffusion and conductivity behavior were analyzed using Nyquist plots and evaluated in the frequency range of 10−2-105 Hz at the open circuit potential. The Nyquist plots are composed of the internal resistance, charge transport resistance, Warburg resistance, and double layer capacitance [56]. Fig. 5f shows the EIS plots of the pure MnO2 and the GQDs@MnO2 electrodes. Both electrodes present similar shapes in two regions, which consist of a semicircle at a high frequency and a straight line at a low frequency. It can be seen clearly that the straight line of the GQDs@MnO2 composite is more vertical than that of the pure MnO2 at low frequency, implying that it has better capacitive behavior [57]. In the high-frequency region, the diameter of the semicircle gives an indication of the charge transfer resistance (Rct). Obviously, the Rct value of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode (6.25 Ω) is lower than that of the pure MnO2 (24.41 Ω), implying that the GQDs@MnO2 electrode has a lower electron transfer resistance across the electrode-electrolyte interface [58], which can be attributed to the GQDs@MnO2 electrode having a network structure and coupling the GQDs on the MnO2 surfaces. Moreover, the intercept value on the X-axis represents the solution resistance (Rs) of the anode at high frequency. The pure MnO2 electrode presents an Rs value of 0.35 Ω, while the Rs value of GQDs@MnO2 electrode is 0.27 Ω, which can be associated with the in situ formed GQDs on the MnO2 nanosheets, indicating good agreement with the results of the IR drop. The long-term cycling performance of the pure MnO2 and GQDs@MnO2 electrodes was examined, and the variation in the specific capacitance value over 6000 cycles at 5 A g−1 is depicted in Fig. 5g. The capacitive behavior of the as-synthesized GQDs@MnO2 exhibits better cycling performance than that of pure MnO2. For pure MnO2, the specific capacitance decreased to 40 % of the initial capacitance after 6000 cycles, indicating the poor cycling stability of MnO2. This can be ascribed to structural collapse, electrode pulverization, and poor conductivity during long-term cycling. In contrast, the specific capacitance of the GQDs@MnO2 electrode retained 80 % of the original capacitance after 6000 cycles. The excellent cycling life of the GQDs@MnO2 may be attributed to the following three aspects: 1) The GQDs can suppress the aggregation of MnO2 nanosheets, which maintain a stable electrode structure during long-term cycling; 2) Loading GQDs on the MnO2 nanosheets increases the wettability and available contact sites, which benefit when reacting with the electrolyte ions; and 3) The Mn-O-C bonds in the GQDs@MnO2 electrode exist to prevent the strain and stress that pulverize the electrodes.

For the sake of further evaluation of their possibility for SC application, an ASC consisting of the GQDs@MnO2 composite as an anode and AC as a cathode was assembled. As shown in Fig. 6a, the CV curves of AC displayed EDLC behavior within the potential window of −1.0-0 V. The assembled ASCs were measured at a working voltage window of 2 V in a 1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The CV curves of the assembled ASC at different scan rates from 1 to 20 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 6b. The resulting CV curves still maintain an approximately rectangular shape even at a scan rate of 20 mV s−1, which indicates excellent reversibility and outstanding stability. It is worth noting that the assembled ASCs were measured at a high operation voltage of 2 V with no obvious polarization phenomenon. Fig. 6c shows the GCD curves of the assembled ASC at different current densities from 1 to 10 A g−1. The GCD curves obtained at different currents reveal good symmetry and show almost linear slopes, implying a rapid I-V response and excellent capacitive behavior for the ASC. Fig. 6d illustrates the Nyquist plot for the assembled ASC. It can be seen that the assembled ASC shows a quite low resistance (1.46 Ω). Moreover, the diameter of the semicircle for the ASC related to the Rct value is small, indicating that GQDs attached to MnO2 nanosheets enhance conductivity and that abundant edge sites are favorable for the charge transfer process. Obviously, the straight line part of the EIS result for the assembled ASC is close to the vertical line, implying that it has good capacitive behavior.

To further explore the long-term cycling stability, the capacitance retention capability of the ASC was investigated by charge and discharge at a current density of 10 A g−1 for 8000 cycles, as shown in Fig. 6e. The capacitance retention of the assembled ASC is 81.3 % after 8000 charge-discharge cycles at a current density of 10 A g−1, which is better than previously reported values [59–61]. In comparison with the performance of the ASCs, the power density and energy density are listed as Ragone plots in Fig. 6f. All the results were calculated based on the total mass of the active materials. With increasing power density, their energy density decreases. It was noted that the energy density of the assembled ASC of AC//GQDs@MnO2 is 29.9 W h kg−1 at a power density of 530.0 W kg−1, which is higher than those of other ASCs with aqueous electrolyte solutions reported in recent years [62–67]. Obviously, the higher energy density and power density of the assembled ASC are due to the unique heterostructures between the GQDs and MnO2, which is consistent with the results from the above discussion. In addition, in the assembled ASC of AC//GQDs@MnO2, the preparation of GQDs@MnO2 in our work is very easy and the Na2SO4 electrolyte is inexpensive. These results indicate that the GQDs@MnO2 composite has excellent electrochemical performance and is a very promising electrode material for SCs with both high energy and good capacitance retention.

In summary, we report a highly efficient one-step to synthesize the GQDs and the GQDs@MnO2 composite by a mild hydrothermal route with the GO and KMnO4 as the reaction reagents in a short time. The resulting GQDs@MnO2 composite is that the GQDs anchored on the surfaces of MnO2 nanosheets. Due to its increment in conductivity, wettability and the available contact sites, the GQDs@MnO2 composite is beneficial when reacting with electrolyte ions, and possesses excellent electrochemical performances when being utilized as an electrode. The assembled ASC based on the GQDs@MnO2 composite and AC demonstrated an excellent energy density of 29.9 Wh kg−1 at a power density of 538.0 W kg−1, as well as the enhanced cycling stability (81.3 % retention after 8000 cycles). This work not only provides a novel approach for synthesizing the GQDs but also offers a new insight into design of high performance aqueous SCs in the future.
